categoryDescription: Evaluates the ETF's risk profile — how bumpy the ride is, how bad the worst losses have been, how well it held up vs peers during stress, and whether the return was worth the risk taken. Pass/Fail is always mandate-based: a high-beta thematic fund is expected to swing, a low-vol fund is expected to be calm, a covered-call fund is expected to trade upside for downside protection, and a managed-futures fund is expected to decorrelate from equities — each is judged against its own job, not against a broad-equity benchmark. Factors are assigned to one or more ETF groups defined in etf-analysis-categories.json; each group uses exactly 5 factors from this file.

factors.factorDescription: Measures how much the ETF's price moves day-to-day and month-to-month. Uses beta (sensitivity vs the broad market, typically the S&P 500 for equity and multi-asset funds) and standard deviation (size of typical return swings). A beta of 1.0 means the fund moves roughly with the market; above 1.2 means amplified swings in both directions; below 0.8 means calmer moves. Interpretation must match the mandate: a 'low-volatility' or 'min-vol' equity fund with beta near 1.0 is failing at its stated job; a 3x leveraged equity fund SHOULD have beta near 3.0 on the underlying and a beta well below that signals tracking breakdown; a multi-sector credit fund has near-zero equity beta by design and equity-beta is the wrong lens — use standard deviation instead. For bond and muni funds, equity-market beta is misleading because the real risk is interest-rate duration — judge via standard deviation of monthly returns and the riskAndVolatilityMeasures block rather than beta alone. For young funds (under 2 years), beta is noisy — flag the short history and lean on category comparison instead of a single number. Pass when the volatility profile fits the fund's mandate and category norm — a high-beta thematic or leveraged fund is Pass if it is delivering the promised swings, a low-vol fund is Pass if beta and standard deviation are materially below the broad market. Fail when volatility is materially mismatched to mandate (low-vol label with market-like beta, leveraged fund far off its target multiple, or volatility well above category peers without a strategy explanation).
factors.factorMetrics: beta, beta1y, beta2y, beta5y, atr, riskAndVolatilityMeasures

factors.factorTitle: Are You Paid Fairly for the Risk
factors.factorDescription: Measures how much return investors earned per unit of risk taken, using Sharpe (excess return ÷ total volatility) and Sortino (excess return ÷ downside volatility). Higher is better, but the threshold is category-specific: broad equity Sharpe above 0.5 is decent and above 1.0 is very good over a multi-year window; core bond and muni Sharpe is structurally lower (0.2–0.5 is normal) because both numerator and denominator are compressed; allocation and target-date funds that smooth the ride typically land 0.5–1.0; alt-strategies and managed futures can have low or even negative Sharpe during equity bull markets yet still be delivering on a crisis-hedging mandate — judge against category peers, not against broad equity. For active funds Sharpe is the honest test of whether the manager's picks added real risk-adjusted value; for passive funds Sharpe vs category tells you whether the index itself was an efficient exposure over that window. For young funds (under 3 years) Sharpe is unreliable and the short history should be stated explicitly. For leveraged and inverse funds, multi-year Sharpe is essentially meaningless because daily-reset decay destroys the risk/return relationship — these funds should be judged on short-horizon suitability, not on a trailing Sharpe number. Pass when Sharpe is at or above the category median over the longest available multi-year window AND Sortino is consistent with Sharpe (no hidden downside story). Fail when Sharpe is materially below category median over a multi-year window without a mandate-based explanation.

factors.factorTitle: Worst Drop and How Long It Took to Recover
factors.factorDescription: Shows the biggest peak-to-trough fall the ETF has had and, where the data allows, how long it took to recover the prior high. Context by asset class: broad equity drawdowns of -20% to -35% hit roughly once a decade (2020 COVID, 2022 rate shock, 2008 GFC); sector and thematic funds can drop -40% to -60% in a bad sector year (tech 2022, energy 2020); core bond funds were hit with -10% to -30% in the 2022 rate spike (long-duration government like TLT was worst at around -31%); high-yield and EM bond funds drew down -15% to -25% in 2020; leveraged 3x funds have routinely drawn down -70% to -90% in bad years and recovered slowly or not at all. The retail question is emotional — could an investor actually hold through that drop without selling at the bottom? The comparative test matters more than the absolute number: a fund that fell harder AND recovered slower than its category peers is the real red flag, while a fund that fell in line with peers was hit by the asset class, not by a fund-specific flaw. Young funds with no 2020 or 2022 test may under-report true downside — flag this gap and judge by strategy design and peer analogues. Pass when drawdown sits within the normal range for the category and recovery behavior was not materially slower than peers. Fail when drawdown is materially deeper than category peers during the same stress window, or when recovery has been notably slower while peers bounced back, or when the fund's structural design (e.g., concentrated single-name or single-country exposure) magnified the drawdown beyond what the category label suggests.
factors.factorMetrics: drawdown, drawdownDates, athChgPercent, athDate, atlChgPercent, atlDate

factors.factorTitle: Is It Riskier Than Similar Funds
factors.factorDescription: Compares the ETF's overall risk score and risk level against other funds in the same Mor category across 3Y, 5Y, and 10Y windows. The key retail question: is this fund taking more risk than its peers, and if so, is it being rewarded for that extra risk in the return column? Four outcomes: above-average risk with above-average return is an acceptable trade when the ratio is favorable; above-average risk without above-average return is a clear Fail — investors are paying volatility with no payoff; below-average risk with similar-or-better return is a strong sign of disciplined risk management; below-average risk with meaningfully weaker return is neutral — the fund is trading return for safety, which is fine for conservative sleeves. For passive index funds, risk vs category tracks the index — do not Fail a passive fund for having category-like risk when it is doing exactly what it is supposed to do; the right comparison is whether it matches the index within tracking expectations. For young funds (under 3 years) peer comparison is noisier — note the caveat and use the longest available window. Pass when risk is at or below the category median, OR the extra risk is clearly compensated by better category-relative returns over the longest available window. Fail when risk sits consistently above the category median across multiple periods without above-average returns to justify it.

factors.factorTitle: How Much of Market Ups and Downs It Catches
factors.factorDescription: Upside capture measures what percentage of the benchmark's gains the fund catches in up-months; downside capture measures what percentage of the benchmark's losses it absorbs in down-months. For broad-equity passive funds both should be near 100% — material deviation in either direction signals tracking issues or unintended factor tilt. The high-value asymmetric pattern is high upside with low downside — that is what smart-beta, min-vol, quality, covered-call, and other defensive strategies promise. Covered-call and derivative-income ETFs should typically show upside capture of 60–80% and downside capture of 50–70%; if upside is near 100%, the options program is not binding and the fund is not delivering its promised income; if downside is near 100%, the options program is not providing the protection it charges for. Managed-futures and long-short funds often show very low capture on both sides versus equity (under 40% each) — that is the low-correlation mandate delivering, not a flaw. For young funds (under 3 years) the sample of up-month vs down-month returns is small — flag this caveat rather than drawing a confident conclusion. Pass when the capture pattern matches the mandate — symmetric near-100% for passive index, asymmetric favorable for defensive strategies, decorrelated for alt. Fail when a defensive or income-strategy fund shows no meaningful downside-capture advantage over a plain-vanilla passive, or when a passive index fund shows persistent material divergence from 100% on either side.

factors.factorTitle: How Much Rate Changes Hurt This Fund
factors.factorDescription: For bond and muni funds, interest-rate risk is the single dominant risk factor. The mechanism: rates up → bond prices down, and the effect is multiplied by duration (weighted time to cash flow). Long-duration government and muni funds (duration above ~15 years) lost -25% to -31% in 2022 when the 10-year Treasury spiked; intermediate-duration core bonds (duration 5–7 years) lost -10% to -15%; short-duration and ultrashort funds (duration under 3 years) lost only a few percent. The same mechanism works in reverse — the long-duration funds that lost most in 2022 gained most in 2019–2020 falling-rate years. The retail takeaway is to match the fund's duration to the investor's holding horizon and rate view; long-duration is a bet on rates falling. For muni funds, duration behavior is similar to taxable bonds of comparable maturity, and state-specific or AMT-exposed muni funds need the same duration check as their national peers. Compare the fund's 2022 loss to the category and index — if the loss was in line with category, the drawdown was asset-class-driven, not a fund-specific flaw. For young funds without a 2022 data point, judge by stated duration versus category norms and the fund's rolling volatility pattern. Pass when the 2022 (or other rate-shock) loss was in line with category peers for the fund's stated duration bucket, OR current duration positioning is within the fund's mandate band. Fail when the fund lost materially more than category peers in rate-shock years without an offsetting credit or yield advantage, OR when current duration positioning is a large unannounced bet that retail holders cannot easily see.
factors.factorMetrics: beta, beta1y, beta2y, beta5y, riskAndVolatilityMeasures, riskPeriods

factors.factorTitle: Risk That Bonds in the Fund Default
factors.factorDescription: Measures the risk that bonds inside the ETF default or get downgraded, using credit-quality breakdown and behavior during credit-stress periods. Credit-quality tiers: US Treasury essentially zero default risk; agency mortgage near-zero; investment-grade corporate (AAA through BBB) mild default risk with price volatility driven mostly by spread moves; high-yield (BB and below) real default risk — during recessions HY funds typically lose -15% to -25% (HYG lost around -22% in 2008); bank-loan funds are senior-secured floating-rate (lower default loss but still credit-sensitive); preferred stock is subordinated, bond-like in rates but equity-like in crises; EM debt layers credit risk on top of currency and sovereign risk. Muni funds depend on state/local issuer health — national investment-grade muni is relatively safe, high-yield muni carries real default risk, and state-specific concentrated muni adds single-jurisdiction risk (pension stress in IL and NJ, revenue shock in oil-dependent states). The stress-test is comparative: compare the fund's losses in 2020 (credit dislocation) and 2022 (rate-plus-spread shock) to its credit-peer category; losses materially worse than peers suggest excess exposure to lower-rated or concentrated issuers that the fund label does not make obvious. For young funds without a crisis data point, read the current credit-quality breakdown and judge against peer norms. Pass when the credit-tier mix and stress behavior are in line with category norms and the fund's stated credit mandate. Fail when losses in 2020 or 2022 were materially worse than category peers, OR the credit-mix is reaching for yield beyond what the fund's labeled category implies.

Daily-reset leveraged and inverse ETFs reset their leverage target every day, which mathematically guarantees that long-term returns are NOT the leverage factor times the underlying's long-term return. In flat or choppy markets daily compounding destroys value (volatility drag); in strongly trending markets it helps. A 3x S&P 500 fund can lose money over a year when the S&P rises, if the path was volatile enough. This factor is partly a structural warning — every product in the group carries it by design — so the retail takeaway to reinforce is always: not buy-and-hold regardless of Pass/Fail. What distinguishes a good leveraged product from a bad one is execution quality: (1) daily-leverage fidelity — a well-run 3x should match 3× the underlying's daily return within a few bps; (2) issuer transparency on holding-period risk; (3) minimal bid-ask slippage given the rapid trading these funds require; (4) no catastrophic structural failures (inverse oil funds in April 2020 when crude went negative, inverse volatility products in February 2018). For young leveraged funds without a full year of data, judge by daily tracking tightness and the issuer's record on comparable products. Pass when daily-leverage fidelity is tight, the fund is clearly marketed as short-term, there is no history of structural blowups, and the category-expected decay is disclosed. Fail when daily tracking breaks down beyond category norms, OR structural issues have produced outsized losses in a specific stress event relative to peers, OR liquidity and bid-ask make intended short-term trading impractical.
factors.factorMetrics: return1m, return3m, return1y, return3y, returnsAnnual, beta, beta1y

factors.factorTitle: How Well It Holds Up in Market Crashes
factors.factorDescription: Downside protection is the core value proposition for covered-call and derivative-income ETFs, managed futures, market-neutral, long-short, and balanced allocation / target-date funds. The question: when equities drop, how much of that drop did this fund absorb? Measured via the ratio of fund drawdown to benchmark drawdown during stress windows (2020 COVID, 2022 rate shock, 2008 GFC) and via downside-capture against a relevant equity benchmark. Rough interpretation: protection ratio below 0.50 is excellent (fund fell less than half as much as benchmark); 0.50–0.75 is good; 0.75–0.90 is modest; above 0.90 means the fund is taking essentially the full ride down and is not providing meaningful protection for its fees. For allocation funds a 60/40 portfolio that fell -20% when stocks fell -20% failed at its core mandate; -12% to -14% would have been in line. For covered-call funds (QYLD, JEPI, SPYI-style), roughly 70% upside capture and 50% downside capture is what the strategy is supposed to deliver — if the fund gave up upside without real downside relief, that is a Fail. For managed futures and long-short funds, the honest test is actually positive or near-flat returns in equity bear markets (the real diversification test), not just a slightly smaller negative number. Young funds without a bear-market test must be judged provisionally using volatility and capture data, with the caveat stated. Pass when the protection ratio is meaningfully below 0.75 in the latest equity-bear window for the fund's mandate. Fail when the protection ratio is above 0.85 despite the fund charging fees for downside protection or balanced-allocation exposure.

factors.factorTitle: Risk of Being All-In on One Theme
factors.factorDescription: Sector and thematic ETFs deliberately concentrate — that is the product — but within-bucket concentration is where the real hidden risk sits. Check three things: (1) top-10 holdings weight — below ~40% is well-diversified within the theme, 40–60% is typical, above 60% means the fund's fate is tied to just a handful of names; (2) single-name maximum — any position above 10% is meaningful single-stock risk disguised as a sector/thematic bet (several semiconductor funds have carried around 15% in NVDA); (3) sub-sector, single-country, or currency concentration within a theme (e.g., a 'China' fund that is mostly Hong Kong-listed internet names, a 'clean energy' fund that is mostly US solar, an 'EM' fund that is 30%+ Taiwan). The asset-class context frames expectations: tech funds lost around -30% in 2022 when tech led down; energy funds lost more than -50% in 2020. Even a 'diversified' thematic ETF should not have single-name risk the category label does not disclose. Pass when within-theme diversification is reasonable for the category — no single-name above ~15%, top-10 weight not above category average — AND the fund's drawdown history during sector down-cycles was not materially worse than its sector-peer group. Fail when single-name concentration exceeds ~15% combined with 'diversified' marketing language, OR top-10 concentration is meaningfully higher than category average and the fund has shown outsized drawdowns because of it. Always reinforce the retail takeaway: these belong as small slices of a portfolio, not the whole thing.
